tuberculosis.characteristics: Mycobacterium tuberculosis infection causing pulmonary inflammation with characteristic cavitary lesions visible on chest X-ray. Affects upper lobes predominantly with possible bronchial spread patterns.
tuberculosis.symptoms: Chronic productive cough lasting >3 weeks, hemoptysis, night sweats, fever, unintentional weight loss, chest pain, and fatigue.
tuberculosis.treatment: Standard 6-month regimen: Rifampicin + Isoniazid + Pyrazinamide + Ethambutol (RIPE). Directly observed therapy essential. Drug sensitivity testing guides regimen modifications.
tuberculosis.prevention: Tuberculin skin test (TST) screening, prophylactic INH for contacts, BCG vaccination, respiratory isolation during infectious phase, good ventilation.
tuberculosis.advice: Urgent referral to TB specialist or infectious disease. Ensure treatment adherence with directly observed therapy. Monitor for hepatotoxicity and peripheral neuropathy. Repeat CXR at 2 months.

pneumonia.characteristics: Acute infection with lobar or segmental consolidation, air bronchograms, and possible pleural effusion on chest X-ray. Interstitial infiltrates common in viral cases.
pneumonia.symptoms: Productive cough with purulent sputum, fever >38°C, dyspnea, pleuritic chest pain, chills, and potential respiratory distress.
pneumonia.treatment: Empiric antibiotics: Beta-lactam (amoxicillin-clavulanate) + macrolide. De-escalate based on culture results. Consider hospitalization if SpO2 <90% or respiratory rate >30.
pneumonia.prevention: Pneumococcal conjugate vaccine (PCV13/PCV20) + PPSV23, annual influenza vaccination, smoking cessation, avoid air pollution exposure.
pneumonia.advice: Assess severity using CURB-65 score. Monitor oxygen saturation closely. Follow-up CXR at 4-6 weeks to confirm resolution. Consider ICU if sepsis suspected.

normal.characteristics: Healthy retinal anatomy with intact photoreceptor layer, normal optic disc with cup-to-disc ratio <0.6, healthy macula with normal foveal reflection, and intact blood vessels.
normal.symptoms: No symptoms. Normal visual acuity (20/20 or better), normal color vision, normal visual fields.
normal.treatment: No treatment required. Continue routine preventive eye care and annual comprehensive eye exams.
normal.prevention: UV protection with sunglasses, omega-3 supplementation, antioxidant-rich diet (leafy greens, berries), manage systemic diseases (diabetes, hypertension).
normal.advice: Maintain healthy lifestyle. Continue annual screening exams. Report any vision changes immediately. Educate on symptoms requiring urgent evaluation.

age-related macular degeneration.characteristics: Progressive degenerative disease affecting central retina. Dry form (atrophic): drusen and RPE changes. Wet form (exudative): choroidal neovascularization with subretinal fluid and hemorrhage.
age-related macular degeneration.symptoms: Gradual central vision blurring, wavy lines (metamorphopsia), dark spots in central vision (scotoma). Wet AMD causes sudden severe vision loss.
age-related macular degeneration.treatment: Dry AMD: AREDS2 supplements (lutein, zeaxanthin, omega-3), zinc. Wet AMD: anti-VEGF injections (ranibizumab, aflibercept, bevacizumab) or photodynamic therapy.
age-related macular degeneration.prevention: AREDS2 supplementation, UV protection, smoking cessation (major risk factor), blood pressure control, omega-3 fatty acids (fish oil), antioxidant-rich diet.
age-related macular degeneration.advice: Monthly Amsler grid testing for early detection. Urgent OCT and fluorescein angiography if sudden vision change. Wet AMD needs anti-VEGF therapy within days of onset.

diabetic retinopathy.characteristics: Progressive microvascular disease: microaneurysms, dot-blot hemorrhages, hard exudates, cotton-wool spots, venous beading. Advances to neovascularization with proliferative disease.
diabetic retinopathy.symptoms: Often asymptomatic in early stages. Progressive: floaters, blurred vision, scotomas. Proliferative: sudden vision loss due to vitreous hemorrhage or traction retinal detachment.
diabetic retinopathy.treatment: Non-proliferative: intensive glucose control (HbA1c <7%), anti-VEGF for macular edema. Proliferative: pan-retinal photocoagulation (PRP) or anti-VEGF (bevacizumab, ranibizumab).
diabetic retinopathy.prevention: Strict glycemic control (HbA1c <7%), blood pressure <130/80 mmHg, annual dilated ophthalmologic exams, ACE inhibitors or ARBs, statin therapy.
diabetic retinopathy.advice: Refer to retinal specialist if any diabetic retinopathy detected. Anti-VEGF therapy is first-line for diabetic macular edema. Fundus photography for monitoring progression.

cataract.characteristics: Opacity of lens affecting visual clarity. Can be nuclear (brown), cortical (white), or posterior subcapsular. May cause glare, halos around lights, and reduced contrast sensitivity.
cataract.symptoms: Gradual painless vision loss, blurred or hazy vision, increased difficulty with night driving, sensitivity to light and glare, faded color perception.
cataract.treatment: Early: stronger glasses/contact lenses, anti-glare coatings. Definitive: phacoemulsification with intraocular lens implant. Surgery indicated when vision affects quality of life.
cataract.prevention: UV protection (sunglasses, brimmed hat), antioxidant supplements, avoid smoking, manage diabetes and hypertension, limit corticosteroid use.
cataract.advice: Refer to ophthalmologist for cataract surgery evaluation. No medication reverses cataracts. Post-operative glasses adjustment needed. YAG capsulotomy if posterior capsule opacifies.

myopia.characteristics: Refractive error: eyeball too long or cornea too curved. Light focuses in front of retina. Fundus shows tilted optic disc, crescent, and myopic crescents.
myopia.symptoms: Blurred distance vision, squinting to see far objects, eye strain, headaches. Near vision typically normal.
myopia.treatment: Correction: eyeglasses, contact lenses, or refractive surgery (LASIK, PRK, ICL). Progression control: atropine eye drops, multifocal contact lenses.
myopia.prevention: Outdoor time (2+ hours daily) reduces myopia progression in children, proper distance from screens (20-20-20 rule), adequate lighting, regular eye exams.
myopia.advice: Regular eye exams to monitor progression. Consider myopia control options for children. Refractive surgery candidates: stable refraction for ≥1 year, age ≥18.

retinal vein occlusion.characteristics: Branch retinal vein occlusion (BRVO) or central retinal vein occlusion (CRVO). Venous blockage causes intraretinal hemorrhages, cotton-wool spots, retinal edema, and ischemia.
retinal vein occlusion.symptoms: Sudden unilateral vision loss or visual field defect, floaters, blurred vision. May be asymptomatic if peripherally located.
retinal vein occlusion.treatment: Anti-VEGF intravitreal injections (ranibizumab, aflibercept), corticosteroid implants (dexamethasone, triamcinolone), laser photocoagulation for macular edema.
retinal vein occlusion.prevention: Blood pressure control <130/80 mmHg, anticoagulation if thrombophilia, carotid screening, diabetes management, smoking cessation.
retinal vein occlusion.advice: Emergency OCT and fluorescein angiography. Assess for neovascularization. Anti-VEGF is first-line for macular edema. Monitor vision closely during treatment.

pneumoconiosis.characteristics: Occupational lung disease from chronic inhalation of mineral dust (silica, asbestos, coal). Progressive pulmonary fibrosis with nodular or irregular small opacities on chest X-ray.
pneumoconiosis.symptoms: Progressive dyspnea on exertion, chronic cough (dry to productive), chest pain, wheezing. Advanced: hypoxemia, cor pulmonale.
pneumoconiosis.treatment: Supportive care, oxygen therapy for hypoxemia, ACE inhibitors for pulmonary hypertension. No cure; focus on preventing progression and complications.
pneumoconiosis.prevention: Workplace exposure prevention: dust suppression, respiratory protection (N95/P100), regular air quality monitoring, health surveillance programs.
pneumoconiosis.advice: Occupational history critical for diagnosis. Restrict further exposure. Annual spirometry and CXR for monitoring. Lung cancer surveillance if asbestos exposure.
